coredata.dc:title: Semi-empirical ageing model for LFP and NMC Li-ion battery chemistries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Nájera, J.
Arribas, J.R.
de Castro, R.M.
Núñez, C.S.
coredata.dc:description: LFP and NMC chemistries are currently, among the Li-ion family, the most relevant and with higher prospects technologies. This paper analyses the modelling process of the capacity decay in a Li-ion battery caused by calendar and cycling ageing. An on-line ageing estimation model valid for both LFP and NMC is developed considering the variation of the main parameters used to define the model: temperature, State of Charge and rate of charge and discharge. This model is validated from a theoretical point of view for both chemistries by comparing its performance with the data provided by manufacturers and previous ageing models. The proposed battery ageing model attains a maximum relative error of 3%, which varies depending on the battery chemistry and the specified working conditions. A further analysis regarding the model accuracy at the battery end-of-life is developed. Moreover, the model performance is validated from an experimental point of view with an NMC battery tested in the laboratory, reaching errors below 5%. In addition, a methodology for parameterizing the ageing model is proposed aiming to facilitate the application of this model into a specific battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ageing
Capacity fade
Experimental validation
Lithium batteries
Semi-empirical model
Theoretical validation

Theoretical validation of the proposed model
Experimental validation of the capacity fade model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Theoretical validation of cycling ageing model for A123 LFP cell in comparison with the datasheet and different authors. Manufacturer data obtained from [33], and authors data from [22,26,34]. 100% DoD and 1 C/1 C for 25 °C, and 3 A/5 A for 45 °C and 60 °C.
Relative error for A123 LFP cell in comparison with the datasheet.
Theoretical validation of calendar ageing model for Sony LFP battery at 35 °C and different SoC.
Relative error for Sony LFP battery at 35 °C and different SoC.
Theoretical validation of calendar ageing model for Sony LFP battery at 70% SoC and different temperatures.
Relative error for Sony LFP battery at 70% SoC and different temperatures.
Theoretical validation of cycling ageing model for Sony LFP battery at 23 °C and different Crates.
Relative error for Sony LFP battery at 23 °C and different Crates.
Theoretical validation of calendar ageing model for 20 Ah NMC battery at 35 °C and different SoC.
Relative error for 20 Ah NMC battery at 35 °C and different SoC.
Theoretical validation of cycling ageing model for 20 Ah NMC battery at 35 °C and different Crates.
Relative error for 20 Ah NMC battery at 35 °C and different Crates.
Theoretical validation of cycling ageing model for 20 Ah NMC battery at 1 C and different temperatures.
Relative error for 20 Ah NMC battery at 1 C and different temperatures.
Theoretical validation of cycling ageing model for SANYO NMC battery at 25 °C and a rate of discharge of 1 C.
Relative error for SANYO NMC battery at 25 °C and a rate of discharge of 1 C.
Experimental validation of cycling ageing model for Kokam NMC battery at 25 °C and a rate of discharge of 1 C.
Test matrix for parameterizing the calendar ageing model.
Test matrix for parameterizing the cycling ageing model.
Obtained values for calendar and cycling ageing parameters for LFP batteries.
Obtained values for calendar and cycling ageing parameters for NMC batteries.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work has been funded in part by the Spanish National Research Agency, Agencia Estatal de Investigación, grant number PID2019-108966RB-I00/AEI/10.13039/501100011033, and in part by the Community of Madrid , that contributed to this work through the SEGVAUTO 4.0-CM (S2018-EMT-4362) Programme.

head.ce:title: Semi-empirical ageing model for LFP and NMC Li-ion battery chemistries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Area of Electrical Engineering, Escuela Técnica Superior de Ingenieros Industriales, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, 28006 Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Area of Electrical Engineering, Escuela Técnica Superior de Ingenieros Industriales, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Area of Electrical Engineering, Escuela Técnica Superior de Ingenieros Industriales, Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, 28006 Madrid, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: LFP and NMC chemistries are currently, among the Li-ion family, the most relevant and with higher prospects technologies. This paper analyses the modelling process of the capacity decay in a Li-ion battery caused by calendar and cycling ageing. An on-line ageing estimation model valid for both LFP and NMC is developed considering the variation of the main parameters used to define the model: temperature, State of Charge and rate of charge and discharge. This model is validated from a theoretical point of view for both chemistries by comparing its performance with the data provided by manufacturers and previous ageing models. The proposed battery ageing model attains a maximum relative error of 3%, which varies depending on the battery chemistry and the specified working conditions. A further analysis regarding the model accuracy at the battery end-of-life is developed. Moreover, the model performance is validated from an experimental point of view with an NMC battery tested in the laboratory, reaching errors below 5%. In addition, a methodology for parameterizing the ageing model is proposed aiming to facilitate the application of this model into a specific battery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growth of lithium-ion batteries in recent years is unceasing because of their excellent features of superb energy density, high voltage cell, low self-discharge, durability, and safety. For these reasons, they are used for many applications nowadays in a wide variety of fields. In relation to electric mobility, the battery is still the most critical element because of its cost and lifespan. The progressive reduction of Li-ion batteries manufacturing costs together with the aforementioned properties help to diminish electric vehicles overall costs and accelerate the switch towards a sustainable mobility. Moreover, Li-ion batteries have an enormous potential as a grid-scale energy storage system. This battery technology is considered well suited to deal with the drawbacks arising from the massive integration of renewable energy sources into the electric power grid [1]. This way, the grid-level Li-ion energy storage system can boost the establishment of a sustainable and low-carbon global energy mix.

Li-ion batteries suffer from degradation caused by their operation and their exposure to environmental conditions [2–5]. This deterioration, called ageing, influences both the aptitude of the battery to store energy, and its capacity to deliver the power requested by the load. Indeed, as the ageing increases, the reduction in both parameters is more pronounced. The monitoring and analysis of the evolution of those two factors at any point along its life is a matter of interest due to the limitations that these variables introduce in the battery suitability for a specific application. Estimation models for measuring battery ageing and the behaviour of the most relevant battery parameters are highly convenient to predict the progression of power capability and capacity, since capacity and power fade cannot be evaluated from ordinary information arising from experimental tests such as temperature, current, voltage, State of Charge (SoC), Depth of Discharge (DoD), etc. [6].

The model parameters vary depending on the battery chemistry. Among the Li-ion family exists a wide diversity of anode and cathode materials, which comprise the battery chemistry. The most common anode materials are graphite and lithium titanate oxide (LTO). In recent years, silicon has also become a prospective anode material due to its superb capacity, ten times higher than that of the used carbon [7]. On the other hand, commercial Li-ion batteries use different cathode materials, such as lithium manganese oxide (LMO), lithium iron phosphate (LFP), layered metal oxide (NMC), and Li rich materials [8]. The majority of anode-cathode combinations available nowadays are LFP/C, LMO/C, NMC/C and NMC/LTO [9]. This document focuses on LFP and NMC chemistries, both with carbon anode, which are considered the most established lithium-ion battery technologies and currently reach around 60% of the Li-ion batteries market share [10].

Unfortunately, capacity lost and ageing are inherent to any kind of Li-ion battery. The ageing process highly depends on the components of the cathode and the anode. As mentioned before, most commercial Li-ion batteries use anode materials based on carbon. However, these materials are highly unstable and the cycling processes gradually lead to the decomposition of the electrolyte, which activates a series of electrochemical processes [11,12] ending in a reduction of the battery capacity and in an increase of the battery internal resistance [13]. Those electrochemical processes are accelerated by high temperatures in the battery during both storage and working states, overcharging, charging with low temperature and cycling with a high depth of discharge [14–16].

The study and evaluation of ageing and capacity fade for Li-ion batteries can be accomplished by using experiment-based estimation methods or model-based methods. The former requires a large number of experiments to reach valuable outcomes [17,18], whereas the latter is not so time-consuming and allows to achieve results in a shorter period. Hence, the estimation method developed in this paper is based on a mathematical estimation model. These models implement a precise methodology to characterize the main variables affecting battery ageing. The major benefits of ageing models lie in the optimization of the operating conditions which can help to enhance the profitability of each specific application.

Battery ageing models have often been classified between models of the detailed chemistry of the cell and empirical models fitting test data to equivalent circuit models [19]. However, and given the high attention received by mathematical models such as neural networks and fuzzy logic, that classification can be expanded to electrochemical models, mathematical models, and equivalent circuit models [20]. The first one predicts the advance of capacity fade by means of partial differential equations, whereas the second one is a self-adaptive model that updates itself depending on the new data received. The third one, also called semi-empirical model (SEM), is based on mathematical expressions to calculate both the ageing caused by the battery utilization or cycling ageing and the degradation suffered by the battery over time or calendar ageing. In this paper, a semi-empirical model that estimates battery ageing in LFP and NMC batteries is presented. These models are widely used and highly suitable for the development of general expressions for LFP and NMC chemistries due to their simplicity, their facility to be parameterized and the limited amount of data required, when compared with other model-based approaches such as electrochemical models and intelligent models. Despite the higher error committed by semi-empirical models in the prediction of capacity fade, it remains below 5%, which is acceptable for most applications [21].

The paper is organized as follows: in Sections 2 and 3 the proposed battery ageing model for LFP and NMC Li-ion batteries is presented. Section 4 shows a methodology to determine the battery parameters required to implement the model into a specific battery. In Section 5 a theoretical validation of the model is developed by comparing the model behaviour in certain conditions with the data provided by battery manufacturers. Finally, in Section 6 an experimental validation process is performed for an NMC battery based on the outcomes of the cycling tests performed at Escuela Técnica Superior de Ingenieros Industriales from Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (ETSII-UPM) facilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LFP and NMC ageing model characterization

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a semi-empirical or equivalent-circuit ageing model for LFP and NMC Li-ion chemistries is developed. These models conventionally distinguish between calendar and cycling ageing.

The proposed ageing model considers in the first place calendar ageing, defined as the deterioration suffered by Li-ion batteries with the passage of time, independent of charge–discharge cycling. Apart from time, mainly two variables affect calendar ageing: temperature and SoC. The way that these parameters influence calendar ageing varies depending on the battery chemistry. Eq. (1) follows an Arrhenius exponential expression and is used to estimate the dependence between the aforementioned variables and calendar ageing, represented by the capacity fade along time [22–24]:
(1)
L n
( ∇
Q
c a l
)
= f ⋅
e
g ⋅ S o C
⋅
e
h / T
⋅
t
z
where
∇
Q
c a l
represents capacity fade due to calendar ageing,
S o C
refers to State of Charge, T is the battery temperature and t denotes time. Furthermore, three constants depending on the battery chemistry ( f , g and h ) are used to adjust the model to each specific battery. Section 4 will show a method to extract those constants from a single battery. Finally, z is known as power law factor and it can take values between 0.5 and 1. In this paper, a value of 0.5 has been chosen for this parameter.

As shown in (1), keeping the battery at higher SoC and temperature, exponentially reduces the battery capacity and lead to an accelerated degradation [22,25,26]. It should be noted that constant h must be negative for this to be possible.

Cycling ageing occurs when the battery is charged or discharged. This degradation mainly depends on the battery operating mode and, specifically, on the selected current profile and working temperature. Moreover, the battery chemistry influences the capacity loss derived from the cycling processes. Even though semi-empirical models apply diverse expressions in the literature aiming to represent cycling ageing, the model presented in this paper makes use of the equation considered most accurate to symbolize this form of ageing [21]:
(2)
L n
( ∇
Q
c y c
)
=
( a ⋅
T
2
+ b ⋅ T + c )
⋅
e
( d ⋅ T + e )
⋅
C
r a t e
⋅ A H
where
∇
Q
c y c
represents capacity fade due to cycling ageing,
A H
refers to ampere-hour throughput, T is once again the battery temperature and
C
rate
represents the charge and discharge current applied to the battery referred to its nominal capacity. As happened in the case of calendar ageing, a series of constants depending on the battery chemistry, a to e , are defined to adapt the model to each specific battery.

Cycling ageing is caused, by definition, by the ampere-hour throughput. Hence, a proportional relation between those parameters is approached [22,26,27]. Moreover, excessively high or low working temperatures deteriorate the power capability and the battery capacity. The parabolic relation properly represents the optimum range of working temperatures, which must be used to avoid the induction of adverse electrochemical reactions in the battery leading to a deeper capacity fade. At last, it is shown that higher Crates accelerate battery ageing in an exponential way [22–24]. Notice that Crate and temperature are coupled in the exponential factor. This dependence stems from the fact that the deterioration rate decrease with temperature, in spite of the higher degradation suffered by the battery as the Crate increases [21]. For this reason, constant d must be negative.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed form for the ageing equation
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the expressions exposed above, this section introduces a new generic equation acceptable for LFP and NMC chemistries. The aggregation of Eqs. (1) and (2) results in an accurate expression for both chemistries, since it embraces the most relevant mechanisms of battery ageing:
(3)
Q
l o s s
=
Q
c a l
+
Q
c y c
.
The presented equation considers the fundamental parameters concerning battery ageing. Considering (1) and (2), the general ageing equation proposed is presented as follows [21]:
(4)
Q
l o s s
=
( a ⋅
T
2
+ b ⋅ T + c )
⋅
e
( d ⋅ T + e )
⋅
C
r a t e
⋅ A H +
f ⋅
e
g ⋅ S o C
⋅
e
h / T
⋅
t
z
where
Q
l o s s
is the final capacity fade in %, T refers to the battery temperature in K,
A H
is the ampere-hour throughput in Ah,
C
rate
is the charge/discharge rate, t represents the time in days, z is the power law factor and, finally, constants a to h are parameters dependent on the battery chemistry.
The capacity loss estimation model devised in last section is valid for LFP and NMC batteries and considers both calendar and cycling ageing.

This model is based on (4). Nevertheless, the applicability of this equation is restricted to constant cycling conditions. This circumstance is not usual in real operating conditions, consequently, a fine adjustment of the ageing model is mandatory. This section tries to fulfil this requirement by contemplating the variation of the main battery parameters: Crate, State of Charge, and temperature.

The proposed solution considers the dependence with time of the aforementioned variables, as shown in (5) and (6) [21]:
(5)
Q

= f
( T

( t )

z
(6)
Q
c y c
= g
( T
( t )
,
C
r a t e
( t )
)
⋅ I
( t )
⋅ t
where f and g are functions considered constant over time ( t ), whereas temperature ( T ), rate of charge and discharge (
C
rate
) and State of Charge (
S o C
) experience variations during the cycling process. On the contrary, since the current flow ( I ) is directly proportional to the capacity loss, the variation with time of the former generates a similar modification in the latter.

At this point, the previous equations are transformed into differential equations by considering a time differential (
d t
). In this infinitesimal period, it is feasible to state with absolute certainty that f and g remain constant. Regarding prior considerations, the elemental variation of capacity fade due to calendar (
d

c a l
) and cycling (
d
q
c y c
) ageing during this brief interval can be represented as follows [21]:
(7)

u =

2
. At last, the integration of (7) and (8) over a finite period comprised between an initial time
t
i
and a final time
t
f
leads to the final battery ageing dynamic model. The addition of the integral formula results in the proposed expression for modelling Li-ion battery ageing, which can be added to the simulation model [21]:
(9)

∫

⋅ z ⋅

(10)

( t )

d t

Note that f and g in Eqs. (5) to (10) are functions and differ from f and g in Eqs. (1) to (4), which are parameters. In a similar way, in Eqs. (1) to (4)
e is a parameter when written in the exponent and corresponds to the exponential function (exp) when not.

The final battery ageing model defined in last section includes certain constants depending on the battery chemistry. The application of the model for a specific battery requires the performance of several laboratory tests in different working conditions with the purpose of capturing fixed values for parameters a to h . It is highly recommendable to execute this evaluation to the battery before simulating the model for the first time and avoid using outcomes from similar batteries. On the contrary, a noticeable reduction on the model accuracy may be experienced and unclear or confusing results may be achieved. Indeed, one of the main advantages of semi-empirical models lies in the reduced testing effort in comparison with other mathematical models.

The major benefit of this process is the achievement of a singular simulation model capable of predicting capacity fade due to both calendar and cycling ageing. The following is a detailed explanation of the procedure for catching the values of the aforementioned parameters according to the considered type of ageing [21].

Eq. (1) contains three parameters depending on the specific battery: f , g and h . As a reminder, calendar ageing varies primarily with temperature, State of Charge and time. For this reason, calendar ageing tests consist of keeping the battery at a concrete temperature and SoC during certain days. Regularly, capacity tests must be performed in order to keep track of the evolution of the State of Health (SoH) of the battery, which is a common indicator used to measure the capacity fade as a percentage of the initial rated capacity.

The following laboratory tests must be accomplished, at least, for parameterizing calendar ageing constants [21]: two tests at a fixed temperature changing the State of Charge and two tests at a different fixed temperature keeping the same values for SoC. Consequently, Table 1 is assembled with the required tests. If possible, a temperature of around 23 ° C to 25 ° C is meant to be one of those testing temperatures, since it belongs to the most suitable range of temperature for Li-ion batteries.

Cycling ageing parameters can be identified applying a similar methodology. Eq. (2) shows five parameters depending on the battery chemistry: a , b , c , d and e . In this case, the main variables affecting ageing are temperature, Crate and Depth of Discharge. Cycling ageing tests are based on the sequential charge and discharge of the battery under different working conditions regarding the aforementioned variables. Periodically, capacity tests must be inserted between cycling tests with the purpose of measuring capacity fade at different moments.

The parametrization of cycling ageing requires the performance of, at least, 5 tests [21]. The identification of parameters a , b and c is achieved by performing three tests at a fixed Crate and DoD using three different temperatures. On the other hand, two tests at a fixed temperature, keeping the previous DoD, but changing the value of Crate is needed to identify parameters d and e . To clarify this idea, Table 2 represents in a simple way the required set of tests, as well as the changes in the key variables influencing cycling ageing.

It is worth highlighting at this point that an increment in the number of tests performed implies a higher accuracy in the parameters identification which leads to an enhancement in the battery ageing estimation. Hence, it is critical to reach a balance solution between model precision and battery deterioration derived from the parametrization tests.

Finally, once the tests have been performed, it is required to collect the parameters values. Each capacity measurement taken from the tests gives an equation, so an over-dimensioned no linear system is obtained. A least square method has been applied for solving the corresponding equation system, so the parameters can be obtained. For the parameterizations carried out in this paper, the least square method has been programmed in MATLAB and solved through the LMFsolve function [28].

As an example, the outcomes of the described testing process can be depicted based on the tests performed by other authors for LFP and NMC batteries. The parametrization procedure for an A123 LFP battery is proven by using the data provided in [24,29] for a 3.3 V and 2.4 Ah cylindrical cell. Additionally, a Sony LFP 3.2 V nominal voltage and 3 Ah capacity is tested based on the data provided by [30,31]. On the other side, authors in [27] state the same information for a Sanyo NMC battery of 1.5 Ah and 3.7 V. A further analysis is made by using the NMC battery proposed in [19,32].
To sum up, the obtained values for calendar and cycling ageing parameters for the aforementioned LFP and NMC Li-ion batteries are shown in Tables 3 and 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical validation of the proposed model
ce:section.ce:para: The purpose of this section is to validate, from a theoretical point of view, the developed battery ageing model, which constitutes the main target of this paper. The purpose is to compare the behaviour of the model developed in Sections 2 and 3 with the capacity loss information provided in real batteries datasheets. A previous stage of parametrization similar to that developed in Section 4 is required to get to know the model parameters for each specific battery. An independent examination is performed for LFP and NMC batteries.
The validation of the capacity fade model is executed independently for calendar and cycling ageing. The following LFP cells are applied in this section:

ce:list-item.ce:para: An A123 LFP cell is selected to validate the cycling ageing model. Data has been obtained from [22,26,33,34].
A Sony US26650FT cell is selected to validate both the calendar and cycling ageing models. This is a 3.2 V nominal voltage and 3 Ah capacity cell. Data have been obtained from [30].

ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the proposed battery ageing model includes certain constants depending on the battery chemistry, which must be characterized prior to the utilization of the model. The selected LFP batteries were already parameterized in Section 4.

Regarding the first battery, a comparison between the model outcomes and the battery datasheet is performed, as shown in Fig. 1. The curves displayed associate capacity fade with number of cycles, showing a reduction of the battery capacity as the number of cycles increases. The operating conditions considered for each test are the following: 2.3 A charge/discharge current for the curve at 25 °C and 3 A/5 A charge/discharge current for 45 °C and 60 °C.

The figure shows a fair model adjustment to the curves provided by the manufacturer. The results obtained for 25 °C present low error, since the evolution of the model capacity fade and the datasheet curve are almost coincident. The model loses accuracy at temperatures higher than 45 °C, obtaining a maximum error of around 2.5% for 60 °C. However, these temperatures are undesirable since they severely shorten the battery lifespan. Consequently, the model performance is satisfactory for the most common temperatures used in industrial applications reaching errors below 1%.

With the objective of highlighting the model accurateness, an additional ageing validation is executed for A123 LFP battery. A deep study of the state of the art encourages the authors of this paper to compare the presented capacity fade model with the results obtained by Wang et al. Petit et al. and Millner in [22,26,34]. These authors employ the same A123 LFP battery for implementing their own equivalent circuit capacity fade model. The comparison between those models and the model proposed in this paper is also presented in Fig. 1 and the working conditions are identical to the ones applied before (relative errors shown in Fig. 2).

The capacity fade progression with the number of cycles proposed in the present model is more precise than the curves presented by the aforementioned authors, since a maximum error of 1.2% for 25 °C and 2.3% for 60 °C is obtained by Wang et al. and Petit et al. whereas the proposed model reaches errors lower than 0.5% for 25 °C and 45 °C. For 45 °C, the proposed model presents a more accurate behaviour than Millner, which reaches a maximum error of 2.3%.

With respect to the Sony US26650FT battery, a complete ageing analysis is implemented, contemplating calendar ageing together with cycling ageing (Figs. 3 to 8). Since the battery datasheet avoids exhibiting the degradation suffered by the battery due to the passage of time, calendar ageing cannot be determined by means of this product sheet. However, there are already many studies in the scientific literature that approach this topic.

Calendar ageing model accuracy is analysed in Fig. 3 and Fig. 5 with respect to the data obtained by Gailani et al. in [30]. The first one shows the influence of SoC and the temperature dependence is displayed in the second figure. As shown in the figures, capacity fade raises with both temperature and SoC. For this reason, these operational factors must be controlled and monitored during storage period to prevent adverse effects. In general, the model performance improves as the days go by, reaching its maximum error for the first 200 days. The relative error in no case exceeds 1.2%, achieving an average error of 0.4%. Therefore, it can be concluded that the model successfully represents calendar ageing for LFP batteries.

Additionally, capacity loss due to cycling ageing is studied for this cell at different Crates. The model prediction with respect to the datasheet curves [31] is shown in Fig. 7. Accurate adjustment is achieved and the deviation diminishes as the number of cycles increases, reaching errors below 2%.
The outcomes accomplished in this section are sufficient to consider the capacity fade model satisfactorily validated for LFP batteries.

A similar methodology is followed for the proposed NMC capacity fade model. The theoretical validation of the battery ageing model is performed with different NMC batteries aiming to obtain a robust model verification:

ce:list-item.ce:para: An NMC cell of unknown manufacturer is selected to validate the adjustment of calendar and cycling ageing model. Data have been obtained from [19,32].
A Sanyo UR18650 W cell is employed to validate the cycling ageing model. Data have been obtained from [27].
A Kokam SLPB100255255HR2 cell is selected to verify the cycling ageing model as well.

ce:section.ce:para: The first analysis is developed for a 20 Ah NMC battery studied within the framework of the European project Batteries2020 and tested by Lucu et al. in [19,32]. The datasheet is unavailable since the manufacturer is unknown. For this reason, the reference curves must be extracted from the aforementioned paper. The values of the model parameters for this battery were shown in Table 4. The validation process developed for this battery comprises the following aspects:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Calendar ageing: the ageing due to the passage of time is predicted by the model as shown in Figs. 9 and 10. The first figure shows how capacity loss due to calendar ageing grows as SoC increases. The model tends to be closer to the reference as time elapses and the average error stands at around 0.6%.

Cycling ageing: Figs. 11 and 13 show the model adjustment with respect to the data collected in [19]. Both figures exhibit an excellent model performance, whatever the temperature or the Crate is. The average error is under 0.5% (see Figs. 12 and 14), which reveals a superb model accuracy and a great adaptability since a wide range of operational factors, temperature and Crate, has been considered.

ce:section.ce:para: The second comparison is based on the SANYO battery model UR18650 W. The required parametrization of this battery was executed in Section 4 and its main constants were shown in Table 4. Figs. 15 and 16 represent the comparison between the capacity fade curve provided by the datasheet and the outcomes attained by the proposed model with the following working conditions: 100% DoD, temperature of 25 °C, charging rate of 0.7 C and discharging rate of 1 C.

The figure observation indicates that the model accuracy is satisfactory from 2000 cycles on, reaching higher precision as the number of cycles increases. In addition, errors below 3% are obtained for the first 2000 cycles, which guarantee an adequate adjustment for the entire battery lifespan.

A third ageing validation for the NMC chemistry is developed by comparing the model outcomes with the data provided by the Kokam SLPB100255255HR2 manufacturer, whose parametrization can be consulted in [35]. In this case, the manufacturer avoids publishing the capacity fade evolution with the number of cycles. Instead, the battery datasheet indicates the following: the capacity fade after 7000 cycles performed at 25 °C, 80% DoD and a Crate of 1 C for charging and discharging is less than 20%. These working conditions were introduced in the proposed simulation model, resulting in a capacity fade equal to 17.98% after 7000 cycles. Hence, the model follows the manufacturers considerations in relation to battery ageing.

A brief review of the results attained in this section may be convenient at this point. Both calendar and cycling ageing models have been tested for several LFP and NMC batteries. One of the key accomplishments in this section consists of achieving errors below 3% in the range of recommended operating conditions for Li-ion batteries. Moreover, this deviation generally follows a decreasing trend as the cycling process moves forward, which significantly enhances the model capabilities.

Continuing with this approach, the number of cycles that the battery withstands before the end of its lifespan, widely known as end-of-life (EOL), can be accurately predicted by this model. It is extensively reported in the literature that Li-ion batteries reach the end of its life when the capacity falls below 80% with respect to the initial capacity.

To illustrate this idea as an example, the 45 °C curve included in Fig. 13 shows that the proposed model is able to predict the EOL with a relative error of 0.5%, since the capacity fade estimation at EOL provided by the model is 80.45% when the reference data lie in 80%. Regarding LFP batteries, the curve at 3.33 C in Fig. 7 demonstrates the model accuracy at EOL for this chemistry, given that the error attained by the battery at this point is less than 0.4%.

To conclude, the model presented in this paper adjusts satisfactorily to the theoretical data provided by Li-ion battery manufacturers in terms of battery ageing for the two most significant Li-ion chemistries employed nowadays: LFP and NMC. As an additional benefit, the model ensures an accurate EOL estimation for LFP and NMC batteries, so it can be employed for lifespan predictive analysis.
Note that, if the model is going to be parameterized with datasets that include severe non-linearities, such as those included in [36], the accuracy of the model could be lower.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental validation of the capacity fade model
ce:section.ce:para: The authors of this paper consider critical the performance of experimental ageing test with the purpose of comparing the tests results with the outcomes obtained by simulating the proposed model at identical working conditions.

The experimental tests were performed in the research facilities located at Laboratorio de Máquinas Eléctricas within ETSII-UPM installations in Madrid. A complete installation for operating rechargeable Li-ion batteries was assembled to connect the batteries with the power grid and control and monitor the charging and discharging processes. The installation was conceived to allow the current flow in a bidirectional way between the batteries and the grid, aiming to create a high efficiency and environmentally friendly process.

The energy storage system is made up of 14 cells of the 55 Ah Kokam SLPB100255255HR2 NMC Li-ion battery. The cells are connected in series reaching a rated voltage of 51.6 V. A detailed explanation about the installation is available in [21].

The laboratory installation was employed for executing battery ageing tests. In order to obtain the capacity fade of the battery as a function of the number of cycles, the following testing methodology has been undertaken.

Most of the performed tests were cycling tests. The goal of these tests was to force an early ageing into the battery. To this effect, 438 cycles between 80% and 20% SoC were performed divided into two sets of tests with the following conditions [21]:

ce:list-item.ce:para: From 21/06/2018 to 19/11/2018: 201 cycles at 0.5 C charge (27.5 A) and 1 C discharge (55 A) and ambient temperature of 25 °C ± 3 °C.
From 23/11/2018 to 22/03/2019: 237 cycles at 1.5 C charge (82.5 A) and 1 C discharge and a temperature of 25 °C ± 3 °C.

ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, a series of capacity tests were performed periodically aiming to capture the capacity fade as a function of the number of cycles. The performance of these tests was based on the working conditions designated by the manufacturer: a full charge of the battery with a constant current–constant voltage profile (CC–CV) followed by a full discharge at 0.2 C until reaching the cut-off voltage. The tests were completed twice every 50 cycles with the objective of ensuring the required repeatability.

The capacity tests outcomes are compared with the simulation model results in Fig. 17. Three trend lines representing the experimental capacity loss appear in the figure: one for each set of tests performed (coloured in blue and green) and one for the whole set of tests (coloured in red). The deviation of the model estimation regarding the latter is considered highly acceptable since the maximum error obtained is lower than 5%. It is noticeable that repeated capacity tests produce different values of capacity fade. This fact is usual in this kind of experimental tests and trend lines are usually employed to represent the real progression of the battery capacity.

In addition, further benefits of the experimental validation lie in the working conditions considered to carry out the tests: low charge and discharge Crates, temperatures of 25 °C and 60% DoD. These conditions are quite employed in the industry. Hence, the proposed model has demonstrated its efficacy and feasibility in real working conditions, reaching excellent capacity fade and ageing estimations.

To conclude, the cycling ageing model proposed in this paper adjusts satisfactorily to the experimental data obtained as a result of the testing effort and time invested in the cycling process of an NMC Li-ion battery.
In this paper, the authors focus on the development, parametrization and validation of a Li-ion battery ageing model acceptable for the most relevant chemistries nowadays: LFP and NMC.

A general expression is proposed to represent the battery capacity fade due to calendar and cycling ageing in Section 2. The simulation model is modified in Section 3 aiming to take into consideration the variation of Crate, SoC and temperature, which are constantly changing during the cycling process.

Once the final model is attained, an intuitive parametrization procedure is implemented so the model can be applied into a specific battery. The proposed parametrization technique is extensively explained in Section 4.

Section 5 develops a theoretical validation of the model for LFP and NMC batteries. In relation to the capacity fade, the model adjustment for LFP and NMC is considered successful when compared to the theoretical performance detailed in datasheets, since the model deviation stands below 3% regarding the most recommended working conditions for Li-ion batteries. Moreover, the error committed by the model falls as the number of cycles increases. In this regard, a model that accurately quantifies battery end-of-life is a very useful tool for commercial applications.

At last, an experimental validation has been performed in Section 6 by comparing the model performance with the outcomes resulting from the experimental cycling process conducted at ETSII-UPM for an NMC battery. The performed ageing tests showed an alignment between the model results and the real capacity fade measured in the battery, reaching errors below 5% for common working temperatures.

The ageing model presented in this paper has revealed its potential to precisely represent the behaviour of LFP and NMC batteries in terms of calendar and cycling ageing. Thus, the predictive analysis of the ageing of lithium-ion batteries of the main chemistries in the market is possible with this model. Furthermore, its great versatility and functionality confer the model an extensive range of application, embracing a wide spectrum of industrial sectors. The principal beneficiaries of the simulation model might be battery manufacturers, renewable energy companies, power grid operators, distributors and marketers, investors or EVs manufacturers, when performing techno-economical analysis or pre-dimensioning studies involving the battery ageing process.
To sum up, the proposed model makes it possible to predict the evolution of calendar and cycling ageing and the battery end-of-life with low testing effort, which significantly reduces time and cost.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interests.
